steps.title: Recognize the exponential growth pattern
steps.content:
The scatterplot points accelerate upward: dots near $x = 0$–$3$ stay below $200$, while dots at $x = 5$–$7$ jump into the thousands (around $2{,}200$ up to $4{,}800$).

The gap between successive points widens as $x$ grows — the hallmark of exponential behavior. A straight line cannot bend to match this acceleration.

steps.title: Eliminate the linear models
steps.content: Choices A and B both propose straight lines. A straight line forced through the low early points must stay shallow and will miss the late points by thousands of units; a line steep enough to reach $(6.5, 4{,}800)$ will overshoot the early dots by hundreds. Neither can stay close to the curving trend. Eliminate A and B.

steps.title: Pick the balanced exponential curve
steps.content:
Both C and D are exponential curves. The curve in choice C sits above every data point — the data lies entirely below it, so its residuals are all negative (systematic overshoot).

The curve in choice D threads through the cluster: some points sit above the curve and some sit below, so the residuals balance — the mark of a true line of best fit.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
